summary: Gruber follows up on his earlier dismissal of the Zune HD as lacking an app platform by revealing that Microsoft is quietly trying to recruit successful iPhone developers to port their apps to the Zune. A developer of a popular iPhone Twitter client told Gruber that Microsoft offered him 'a bucket of money' to port his app, but he turned them down. Gruber assumes Microsoft approached multiple popular iPhone app developers with similar offers. The post highlights the irony of Microsoft, a self-proclaimed platform company, having to pay developers to build for its device rather than attracting them organically. Gruber solicits tips from other developers who may have received similar pitches.
key_points: Microsoft is attempting to bootstrap a Zune HD app ecosystem by paying established iPhone developers to port their apps
At least one prominent iPhone Twitter client developer was offered 'a bucket of money' by Microsoft but declined
The software platform and app ecosystem has become the key competitive differentiator for media devices, not just hardware specs
Microsoft's approach of paying for ports contrasts sharply with the organic developer enthusiasm driving iPhone app growth
money_quotes: Its biggest shortcoming is that it's just a media player and web browser; no apps, no games.
The software platform has quickly become a major driving force behind the sales growth of the iPhone and iPod Touch.
Microsoft is a software platform company. "Developers developers developers", right?
He was contacted by Microsoft a few months ago, with an offer to port his app to the Zune in exchange for "a bucket of money".
He turned them down, but assumes, as I do, that Microsoft reached out to the developers of multiple popular iPhone apps.
key_insight: Microsoft's need to pay iPhone developers to port apps to the Zune reveals just how decisively Apple had won the platform war for mobile apps.
